content:
There’s no “opting out” of AI. The only option is to automate your work away

5 min read
Just now

--

These were my thoughts/feelings in early 2026. I no longer feel/think this way, despite the progress in AI, but I was quite happy with what I wrote and I didn’t want to discard it or rewrite it. I also know many readers will resonate with it. I plan to write a rebuttal soon.
If you are reading this, chances are your job involves the creation of some form of digital work —code, forecasts, reports, articles, music, websites, designs. Congratulations, you’ve just boarded a train you didn’t choose to. One that keeps getting ever faster. The destination? Joblessness. A crushing feeling of uselessness.
You are soon to be dispensed with. You’re about to find yourself in a society where you have nothing to contribute, at least not in the way you were used to, or worse, the way you enjoyed.
How will it feel? Well, think of a pensioner, without a pension. But unlike a pensioner, which can take pride in having devoted a lifetime to the betterment of society, you will be made redundant by an algorithm, one that can do what you do faster, better and cheaper.
So, what are your options?
You could gain some more skills or enhance your productivity in an attempt to outsmart the machine, hoping to remain valuable in the AI era. This is like running backwards on the train, you may delay the arrival, but…
